Earning multiple Nobles on one turn.body:
According to the printed rules in my box, if I buy a gem bonus that makes me eligible for more than one noble to visit, I choose one and play continues. 

What isn't made clear though is what happens on my NEXT turn. Assuming no other player has earned a visit from the noble in question in the interim, do I automatically trigger a visit from that noble as I was (and remain) eligible from the previous turn? Or am I barred from accessing that noble for the rest of the game? 

Apologies if this has been answered elsewhere in the threads, but I couldn't find it!
In the scenario you describe you get the second noble on your next turn.

lots of bgg is not intuitive so it has to be discovered... here I used the search while on the rule forum and I searched for

noble -re:

the -re: usually strips out replies and looks at only the base post of a thread

Reserved cards - face down ?.body:
Hi, everyone.
Could not find it in the forums: is it allowed to keep your (up to three) reserved cards face-down ? Or are they supposed to be "public info" all the time ?
We have always played keeping reserved cards face down.  I will double check the rules in my copy.
I believe that they have to be face-down until they are purchased. This is to separate them from the player’s pool so they aren’t “accidentally” used to purchase cards during gameplay.
According to the rules, reserved cards "are kept in hand". We always interpreted this as being hidden from the other players.

So in my copy, nothing in the rules explicitly states reserved cards must be kept face down. However when it discusses how to reserve a card, it does mention that one method is to reserve from the top of a deck and do not show the other players the card.

(Just following up on what the rules show.)
We keep them face up since it was open information when they were purchased. But there is no justification for that in the rules.

The rules are ambiguous. My spouse and I find it more strategically satisfying to keep them face up. Face down would remove a lot of the most interesting player interaction and jockeying for position, in my view.
We've always interpreted that the reserve cards are face-down.

Oliver is right: the rules states that the card are kept in hand therefor are not public information.

(Side note: it is not fair play to take a visible card from the tableau before all players have seen it)

The rules on reserving a card:

The vast majority of games in which players have a "hand of cards" means that those cards are hidden from other players.  Otherwise they would be called something other than a "hand of cards."  If they were meant to be face-up on the table, they would be called something else, such as your "pile of reserved cards."  But the general consensus in the forums seems to be that reserved cards are face-down (in your hand), and your purchased cards are face-up (in your tableau).  If they were *all* kept face-up on the table, there is a much greater chance of mixing up the cards that you have already purchased with the cards that you have reserved but not yet purchased.

The Splendor rules make it clear that the cards you reserve from one of the face-down decks remain hidden.  There are other games where you are also able to draw from either a face-up pile, or a face-down pile, and the drawn cards go into your hidden "hand of cards" (Ticket to Ride springs immediately to mind).  In that game, as well as in Splendor, it would be too much trouble to separate out the Hidden but Trackable Information (HTI).  In Ticket to Ride, it makes little sense to keep a *hidden* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-down deck, and a separate *visible* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-up offering.  I feel the same way, to a lesser degree, about the reserve cards in Splendor.

In my games of Splendor, *all* reserved cards go face down.

Also, much more reading on the subject, here:

Also, from this six year old thread (), we have this:

Cheers!

Thank you, guys !
We will try face-down / hidden reserved cards, it makes sense for all the reasons you stressed. If my group disapproves it, we can always house-rule it otherwise.

Is gold tied to the card you reserved?.body:
Is gold tied to the card you reserved? 
I seem to recall that it wasn't and you can use the gold as a wild in any later turn to buy cards
But i played it today, and the owner was adamant it is tied to the card - and that you only use the gold as a wild if you managed to buy the reserved card another way; in which case the gold can be used.
I read the latest rules (although very briefly) from the publishers site but wasn't able to find it (probably from skim reading it)

Can someone confirm whether gold is tied to reserved cards or not, and if it is; how do you use gold as a wild for other cards you wish to purchase?

Thanks

Gold is indeed wild and can be used toward buying any card.  For example, if a card costs 1 green and 1 red, you can buy it with 1 green and 1 gold.  Or buy it with 2 gold.

Remember, too, that one cannot have more than 3 reserved cards.

no, you can use it on any purchase

and it does count against the 10 limit at end of your turn
Thats great,  i knew i had it right.  Thanks all

no, you can use it on any purchase

Correct. This is a common misunderstanding, and something I call out specifically when I teach the game.

Rules Complexity and Strategic Complexit.body:
Someone invented a "next-generation way of quantifying the difficulty of a game. A simple 1-5 rating in the same fashion as BoardGameGeek doesn’t tell the full measure of the difficulty of a game since it doesn’t identify if a game is difficult due to the rules or the strategy.

 is a two-dimensional rating scale where the rules difficulty and the strategic difficulty is measured individually.

The first number quantifies the rules complexity using a 1-5 scale. A 1-rating has very few rules and can be taught verbally in seconds (think: tic-tac-toe rules). A 5-rating has a very thick rules manual, many addendums, FAQs, rules charts, player aids, and a long rules explanation. (think: High Frontier)

The second letter quantifies the strategic complexity using an A through an E. An A-rating has very low strategy and may be either instant to grasp or be entirely luck-based. An E-rating has layers and layers of complexity and may take years to master. (think: Go or many 18xx titles)"

I've created a poll to see what other people think the rules complexity and strategic complexity would be for this game.

Geeklist with all Rules Complexity and Strategic Complexity Polls: https://boardgamegeek.com/geeklist/354554

End Game Scoring and Nobles.body:
I apologize if this is covered somewhere else, but there are too many threads and I could not locate anyone discussing the following question I have.

Simply put, do you keep the score you had at the end of your turn, or at the end of the game?

For Example:

3 player game.  Players A B C.  Player A had the first turn.
On Player A's turn, they manage to buy a card that also causes a noble to visit them on their turn, their total is 16 points (Horay!).  End of game triggered, everyone else goes since you play an equal number of turns.
Player B goes, they buy a card, their turn ends, they have 15 points (second place, dang).  
Player C goes, they also buy a card, and additionally, they have enough cards now that they can have the same noble visit them that player A took.  So they take that card for the additional 3 points, however they still only have a total of 14 points.  

Here's my question.  Since Player C took the noble that visited player A, is their score now 13 points instead of 16 points, meaning player B would actually win with 15 points?  Or does Player A win because on their turn they had 16 points.

Thank you to anyone who can explain the official ruling.  Originally I would play where the score would be reduced but we found that this made it more favorable to whoever went last in our games.
Nobles aren't taken away from players.  The first one to meet the required gems takes the noble at the end of their turn.
only 1st player to claim a noble scores points for it, they actually take the noble and put it in front of them
ninja'ed!  just wanted to add that even if you meet the requirements for more than one noble on your turn to claim, one can only claim one noble per turn
OK wow I can't believe we've been playing this wrong the whole time.... my family is going to be so upset with me

Cars required to gain more than one nobl.body:
My question is can the cards be used to obtain multiple Nobles 

In the picture attached the player has received one Noble that requires three green three blue and three red 

There is another Noble available that requires three Brown three red and three green 

Would the player be able to obtain this Noble as well if they add three Brown to their collection of cards, but using the exact same cards for the red and green they needed to obtain the first Noble?

You can obtain multiple nobles using the same cards. You're not spending resources. As long as you satisfied the requirements, you can get the nobles. The only limitation is that you can't get more than one noble per turn.
I'd say it's an important part of strategy to look for nobles with overlapping requirements and try to get multiples.

Can you take two gold if there are 4 or.body: Or can you only gain gold by reserving cards?

You can never take more than one gold on a turn.  The only way to acquire gold chips is use the option of reserving a card and taking a single gold chip.  A player can hold no more than 3 cards in reserve.  Gold is not acquired by taking chips from the bank the way other colors are obtained.
Man, if you could take two gold if there were four or more, the first player advantage would be insurmountable.
you also get no gold if you reserve a card and the gold supply is empty

You can take anything you want if your opponents aren't looking.

:b

And eat it. 
You could also be also victorious by making offers the other players can't refuse.

And eat it. 
You could also be also victorious by making offers the other players can't refuse.

This is what's missing in abstract games, the true cutthroat nature of busines!

Just like in 7 wonders when you "defeat" someone in battle, subsequently leaving their entire city and economy intact! ;)

Resolving a missed hand limit..body:
Suppose a player has more than ten tokens at the end of their turn, but nobody noticed. What rules do you guys use to resolve this when somebody does notice at a later point in time?

We are playing casually and don't want to punish the player too harshly if it was an honest mistake.
When playing casually: Ask them to return the extra tokens when you notice it.
If we notice at their next turn, then they have to get rid of the excess before playing.

It's tricky, depending on *when* you notice.

Suppose its during a 4-player game.  Player 1 takes too many tokens, and no one notices at the time.  Players 2 and 3 take their turns.  Then the error is discovered on Player 4's turn, and the excess tokens are returned.

But what if that would have altered Player 2's and Player 3's turns?  Perhaps Player 2 needed red, which wasn't available at the time, and Player 1 returns a red token when the mistake is discovered during Player 4's turn.

I guess you need to try to redo the last two turns as well.  Or at least allow Player 2, then Player 3, to exchange tokens (assuming they took tokens on their turns), based on the tokens that Player 1 just returned to fix their error.

Cheers!
Yes, when them having the extra tokens in principle limited others players, just ask if they'd have done something differently.
To prevent this, we're in the habit of announcing our chip count at the end of our turn when it gets above five. That prompts us to count every time.

Korean Pokémon Rules Translated to Engli.body: Took pictures of the rules through google translate and combined them in Photoshop. Not perfect and some of the translations are funky but you should be able to learn the game from it.

Differences from the original that I can tell:

18 points wins. If tied, the player with more trained cards (upgraded pokemon) wins.

Cards can be bought with balls or evolved (upgraded) if the player has the corresponding balls in tableau.

Legendary cards need at least one master ball (equivalent to gold in the original).

Yep, and the noble system is sort of replaced by evolving. The game also comes with Pokedex sheets so you can track what Pokemon you have caught over multiple games. I don't remember Splendor having any system for kidnapping merchants.

Pete (thinks that makes sense)

I do not think that's a valid reason to pass on a noble even if you could per the rules. There are only two options how this round can play out:

a) Someone is in turn order before you. They either will already have 15+ points which means it is the last round. Or they have fewer than 15 which means you will win with the noble. 

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.  

Maybe you are forgetting that all players get an even number of turns, meaning the last player will be next to the starting player, not the one next to the player ending the game?

Are the other players here also required to point that out to the player?  

Seems like such a rule should only be in the "family friendly variant" section

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.

Hi!,

The rules in spanish say:
(and my confusion starts with the Game Start part, cuz I don't understant that like a "THE ROUND starts with..." 🥲)

Game Start part: "THE GAME starts with the youngest player and proceeds clockwise".
Game Over part: "When a player has 15 points (don't forget to count the nobles), THE ROUND in play continues, so that all players play the same number of turns."

Maybe I take (for my very notorious lack of reading comprehension) "THE ROUND in play" as from when a player gets the 15 points (or more) that trigger the end of the game and for me all the players take one last turn even if I'm the player at the right from the player who starts "the game" or... "the round..." x_x.

A note on terminology: In Splendor, a *Round* consists of all players taking a single *Turn*.  This is mostly consistent through most board games, but is not always the case.

Say you are playing a 3-player game, with players A, B, and C.  Player A goes first, followed by Player B, and Player C goes last.

Say, Player B triggers the end game by scoring 15 points on their 12th turn.  That means you will finish out the round and the game is over.  Player C will get one more turn (as it will be their 12th turn), and scores 16 points.

Player A doesn't get another turn, since they already took their 12th turn during the 12th and final round before Player B triggered end-game.

End result:  Player A finishes with fewer than 15 points on 12 turns.  Player B finishes with exactly 15 points on their 12th turn.  Player C wins the game by passing Player B in scoring on their final (12th) turn.

Games don't always last 12 rounds, but that seems to be somewhere around the average when I play the app.  Some games end in 10 rounds (or less).  Some end in 14 rounds (or more).  But the main takeaway is that you compare scores after all players have played the same number of rounds.

--As an aside, when playing the app and you reach the prerequisites to earn two Nobles on the same turn, it shows you which two Nobles you can claim, and you must choose one of them.  You will earn the other one automatically at the end of your next turn (provided someone else doesn't claim it before you).  So it is always good to look at other players' tableaus and first choose the Noble that other players are closer earning, so that the second Noble will most likely still be there next round for you to claim.  It does not let you *not* take a Noble that you have earned.

In brief: The player sitting to the right of the youngest player will always take the last turn of the game. (Assuming your started the game according to the rules.) 

For scoring, it does not matter where in turn order the player ending the game is as everyone gets the same number of turns (which will equal the number of rounds played).

Hi! Thanks! and yeah I understand that about the rounds, I have seen it in other games as you mention, but the wording of the rules in Spanish for Splendor confused me “a bit”haha.
So from now on I'll interpret it that way 😀
almost never... for gaves with a start player advantage or disadvantage it will always be the same start player each time for a given game within a small play group.

A few debates to settle (Gold/Nobles).body:
Hi there !
I played with a group for the first time tonight. They played before, me too, but we had a few rules debates. 
Can you settle those for us, please?

First, gold tokens.
You get a gold token when reserving a card, but when can use it:
A) whenever you buy a card
B) only after buying the card you reserved 

Second, nobles.
A) you can have only one noble per game
B) you can get one noble per turn

Third, nobles again.
A) you need a noble to win the game
B) you can win without having a noble

What do you think?

Gold tokens.
You get a gold token when reserving a card, you can use it:
A) whenever you buy a card

Nobles.
B) you can get one noble per turn

Nobles
B) you can win without having a noble

I think the rules are very clear, so maybe someone's been using some house rules.

Reserve card: Visible or no?.body:
I don't know if this really matters in the grand scheme of things, but what's the rule on whether or not your reserved cards are visible to other player(s)?

The way the game was taught to me is that players keep their reserved cards face down, not visible to other players. Now, obviously, if a card is reserved from the face-up cards in play, the other players will easily be able to see what was reserved. However, if a player chooses try to "get lucky" and reserves a card from deck, the rules specifically say to NOT show the other players what the card was. I don't see any other rules about whether or not reserved cards should be kept face up or face down, but I think it was that rule about now showing the card pulled from the deck to other players that caused my friend to interpret the rules to mean that players should not keep their reserved cards revealed to other players.

Like I said, I don't know that it really matters one way or another (though I guess there could be some strategy to keep reserved cards secret). But now that I've added Splendor Duel to my collection, and it has similar rules for reserved cards, it'd be nice to have some definitive clarification.

Here's the answer from Space Cowboys themselves from 2018:
I am writing with a Splendor rules question. It is not at all clear whether, when reserving a card, whether you may, may not, or must not, leave the card face up, or turn it face down.

My interpretation of the rules is that cards reserved from the top of the deck stay face down, and that cards from the play area are face down.

It looks like there's nothing clear about this rule on BGG:
https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/1308007/hiding-your-reserved-cards
https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/1575934/do-cards-reserved-blind-remain-blind

Is there an *official* rule here?

Thanks!
-Robert

Hi Robert,

Reserving a card means taking it for yourself in a way that prevents other people from:
- Seeing it afterwards.
- Confusing it with the cards you already acquired.

It must also be very easy to keep track of the number of cards you have in reserve, and their level.

Hence, yes, keep the cards as if you had them in hand. For all purposes, they work in this way.

Kind regards

François Doucet
Space Cowboys

there's also a thread where someone was trying to address an issue where one of their players deliberately quickly covered and grabbed the card they wanted to reserve so noone else could see it as they picked it up...(eg they "slammed" their hand over it). 

https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/1308007/hiding-your-reserved-cards
Oh yeah, that's a good thread.

We play that reserved cards that are in the table remain face up for all to see.  I have ADHD and my short term memory is terrible.  This is publicly trackable information and in this game, to hope you win because someone forgets or because they have a learning disability is not the way I am sure people want to win by.

This could be a whole separate conversation about making accommodations for people with learning disabilities to make a game fair but I digress.

Second if a player reserves a card from the face down pile that card remains hidden to the other players because this is hidden information.

Gold coin.body: What do you do with gold coin after you reserve a card? Can it only be used with that card or does it become a 'wild' coin and be used as you wish?

The second one.
Right.  And remember, a player can still reserve a card when there are no gold chips in the bank, as long as the player's reserve card total is no greater than three.
Tx. Can't find the answer in the rules.

The following is from the 2014 Space Cowboys edition of Splendor:

(under Reserve a development card)
Reserving a card is also the only way to get a gold token (joker).  If there is no gold left, you can still reserve a card, but you won't get any gold.

Buying a development card
To purchase a card, a player must spend the number of tokens on the card.  A joker token can replace any color. The spent tokens (including any jokers) are returned to the middle of the table.

We always played that you could only use it as a joker for that card because our instructions showed the coin placed on the card to reserve it.we had many 'family discussions' as to how to interpret the rule. TX for the clarification.
That one I didn't know! That helps.
It's important to keep the gold tokens together with your other tokens to ensure that you don't accidentally exceed the limit of ten tokens.
Picture examples complement rules, don't overwrite them, always read the rules as first source.

It's important to keep the gold tokens together with your other tokens to ensure that you don't accidentally exceed the limit of ten tokens. 
We have always kept our colored tokens on top of our same-colored cards, in five side-by-side columns. And we have kept our gold tokens on top of our face down reserve cards (in a sixth stack to the right of our colored cards). That way they are all in a line, and the tokens are easy to count at a glance. And since like colored tokens are kept on top of like colored cards, it is easy to see our total buying power at a glance as well.

I suspect many players organize their chips and cards in a similar way.

EDIT to add... That's not to say that the gold coins can *only* be used on the Reserve cards (they can be spent on *any* card purchase as has been pointed out above). That's just how we organize them so that they are easy to count.  

Cheers!

It's important to keep the gold tokens together with your other tokens to ensure that you don't accidentally exceed the limit of ten tokens. 
We have always kept our colored tokens on top of our same-colored cards, in five side-by-side columns. And we have kept our gold tokens on top of our face down reserve cards (in a sixth stack to the right of our colored cards). That way they are all in a line, and the tokens are easy to count at a glance. And since like colored tokens are kept on top of like colored cards, it is easy to see our total buying power at a glance as well.

I suspect many players organize their chips and cards this way.

Cheers!
That works.  I always keep my tokens with the like colored cards and in line abreast, and the gold tokens in line with them.  Whatever works. 🙂

Receiving Nobles: Cards plus Chips?.body: When meeting the requirement for a Noble. Is it the total of Coins + Cards, or just Cards.
Just cards. The square shape of the icons on the nobles is a reminder of that.

Jewel hand limit.body:
Let me preface this by saying that I play exclusively with two players. 

So I had some confusion about the rules. Somewhere I got the idea that you could only have seven jewels at a time, whereas the rules say you can have ten. Maybe I got it confused with Jaipur. So we played with a hand limit of seven for a few months until last night I was reading the rules and saw that the limit is ten. So last night we played with a limit of ten. But I found myself hoarding jewels, and there were (of course) fewer jewels to pick from. I felt that it may worked better with a lower hand limit than ten. Maybe seven is too low, but I think ten may be a bit too high.

I've also played rules wrong and when finding out I was, still preferred my original option. I submit you're probably playing a different game now.  Hoarding is most definitely a part of it. But, you know, you do you.

My wife and I have played it many times and think that 10 is a good number at every player count.  

Was hoarding jewels your strategy?  Did you concentrate on a couple of colors?

I've found hoarding more effective in a 4-player game.  If 2-3 players are hoarding, the game gets tight.

I also play exclusively with two players, and 10 provides a wide range of strategies. Hoarding gems is a critical part of the game, which allows players to develop Level 2 and Level 3 cards more rapidly. I believe that allowing 10 gems would ultimately speed up the endgame and emphasize the race aspect of the game.

It would become a completely different game with 7 limit. You'd have a strong need for tier 3 cards to be able to advance, something that doesn't happen with the 10 gem limit, since you can go straight up to tier 2.

Not sure which one's better, but I think the game is intended to allow for more options (10 gems), instead of locking you on completing tier 3 cards from the start.

I assume you mean the green-backed cheap Tier 1 cards here, not the blue-backed expensive Tier 3 cards.

But I agree, limiting it to seven gems in hand would be much too restrictive in regards to viable strategies.

At the end of your turn you must give back coins so that you have 10 or less. Gold counts as a coin. You can have 10 coins and draw 3 more on your turn (different colors) or 2 of the same color (as long as there are 4 or more jewels of that color present before you take any). Just make sure you end up with 10 coins at the end by discarding.

Splendor newbie question.body:
Hi everyone, please, help a rookie!

So let's say I want to buy a card that costs 5 emeralds.
I have 5 emerald cards and 2 emerald gems. 
In this situation do I 
1) pay with my 5 emerald cards and keep my 2 emerald gems
2) pay with my 2 emerald gems plus 3 emeralds cards?

In other words, do I always have to use gems first and then cards?
Or I can decide to keep however many gems I want?

Thanks a lot, I love Splendor and Boardgamegeek!

You decide what to do.  I recommend using your 5 emerald cards and saving your 2 emerald gems in order to buy a card requiring 6 or 7 emeralds on a later turn.  Remember:  you expend gems but you keep cards.

Considering that you cannot hold more than 10 gems from the end of one turn to the next, you can always get rid of any emerald gems that you feel are unneeded when you take 2 or 3 new gems from the supply. You can take more gems putting you over 10, but you have to get rid of enough to get back down to 10 before your turn ends.  In other words, let's say that I have 10 gems including those 2 emerald gems.  I take 2 ruby gems from the supply, raising my total to 12.  To get back down to 10, I return the 2 emerald gems to the supply.   That's my turn.

For me, I'd go with keeping the 2 emerald gems and just using the 5 emerald cards.  It's what we call a 'free card' in our Splendor games because you are actually spending nothing.

If you like Splendor, check out .

That makes sense, and that's what I thought!
Thank you for confirming that and I appreciate your fast reply because we played yesterday and we're going to play right now!
Hello from Japan and have a great day!
Yep, some people forget to read the rules :-(.

I don't think the rules mention any kind of choice in that matter.
Card bonuses give discounts and the remainder must be paid with tokens.
TBH I can't think of a situation where it would be beneficial to voluntarily overspend.

Just to be clear:  You don't turn in your cards once you use the gems on them.  They are permanently in your possession.

You use the gems on them many times.

Your "pay with my cards" terminology made me wonder if you got that.

You're right, "pay with cards" sounds like I return the cards.
No, the cards stay in my "wallet" forever and I use them as gem value repeatedly throughout the game. 
Thank you!

What to do when you can't play?.body:
During a game of Splendor on bga, I reached a state where no move cant be made. My timer ran out and I was expelled from the game.

https://i.ibb.co/MCfd7Kg/image.png

* I can't reserve a card because my hand is full.
* I can't take any token because none are available
* I can't spend any card because I cant pay them

How this kind of state should be handle by the game? What should I have done while in this state?

IRL I would just pass. But the rules don't cover this.

However, it looks like you can buy two of your three reserved cards. What am I missing?
I can't buy any reserved cards. I miss on 1 green and 3 blue for the first one and the second one need one more source of green.
By the way, you might do better asking this question on the BGA forums: https://boardgamearena.com/forum/viewforum.php?f=389
You could have played your first reserved card. You have 3 blue gems (2 chips plus 1 from a card) and 1 green gem (1 green chip plus 1 from a card) in your My Gems
You do have 3 blue and 2 green

That interface is awful.  I can't make heads or tails of what the person has/doesn't have or even what is on the cards.  Just mysterious.  I think the numbers on the cards are what is still needed to pay the cost, and blank color means it is possible to be paid?

Moreover, how did the person get in this situation?  Seems early game, has reserved three cards, no gold and I think only has two played cards?  I'm assuming the played cards must be from the middle row but not sure.

I think the player (presumably everyone) is going for nobles but red looks fantastic for this board considering the top row.
Yeah, not very easy to see the card engine he has, he needs to open the eye corner function for details. EDIT: unless he didn't know they grant credit to buy along gems.
He used the golds to buy those two cards.

Out of curiosity I just played around with the Splendor tutorial on BGA and the "eye" button just toggles between showing you how many gems it takes to buy a card vs. how many more gems you need to buy the card. (That's why in the screenshot some of the gems on cards are blank. That's because OP already has the required gems for purchase.)

Also, what's not shown in the screenshot is on the right side there is a display for each player that makes it much more clear which cards and gems they own of each color.

[-]Regardless, OP could have bought either of the first two cards in the reserve.[/-]

Nevermind. Duh. Because the "eye" button is toggled, the cards are showing which gems the OP needs but does not have.

This is a bug, not just a "question to ask" - it should be reported here: https://boardgamearena.com/bugs?game=1394

EDIT: I just reported it: https://boardgamearena.com/bug?id=68933

Here is the official answer from Asmodee:  - in this rare situation, you must pass.
BGA should automatically skip your turn.
Well done going the last  mile, Ori.
Thank you for opening a bug in the bugtracker and sharing the link.
In BGA Splendor I have several times got into a state where I could afford a reserved card but the game wouldn't let me buy it or indicate it was available. F5 / reset has fixed it every time for me.

Looks like there's still a problem related to that, and it's not fixed by a refresh.
And reading your discussion about the reserved card, I think this is the actual bug rather than "can't play anything"

Joker - Single Use or Multi Use?.body:
When you have a Joker (gold coin) in your possession and you want to use it to buy something, does the Joker go back to the house after you buy something, or can you just keep it and use it over and over?

Thanks!

goes back into the pool...

... otherwise everyone's early game would be reserving 3 cards asap to get 3 of them
Here is the relevant quote from the rulebook, found under 'Buying a development card':

do chips have to be spent first?.body:
When paying for new cards, and I have more than tje cost of one gem color when adding my development cards and my chip tokens, do I have to spend out the chip tokens first?

This is how we played in order to keep chips available and push people to play for the higher value cards.  And we find this works very well.

But we wonder what is the official rule.  

To clarify, an example wpuld be that the card I want requires 5 red gems.  I have 3 tokens and 4 development cards.  

Do I have to spend, return, the 3 red tokens first, and then not fully utilize the development cards?  Or do I max out the development cards and only spend 1 token, thereby keeping two in my reserve?

TIA
Tom

The cards work as discounts or virtual currency.  So if a card costs five and you have 4 cards, it costs you one gem.

Edit Beaten by a literal second.
What part of the rulebook made you think that this could be the way of play?

2 things.  First the rules didn't explicitly staye that amd we got the impression that chip hoarding is a negative factor oft discussed, so inentives to keep chip liquidity made sense.

Second is the card symbolism.  It is notable that the noble cards use rectangles to show their costs, ie. Use cards to meet tje requirement, while cards showed circle for their cost, which we took to imply use chips primarily.  

We'll try the proper rules play.  But I could see us returning to the system we used as play has. Even good, balanced, and never an issue with chip hoarding as they must go back into the pool each time a buy is made.
There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique
There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique

Chip hoarding is *not* a negative.  It is an important strategy to help keep your opponent's buying power in check.

The rulebook does indeed cover this.
The rulebook does indeed cover this.

It says you *must* use your card discounts first, before spending any chips:

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!

This.

Bonuses give you a discount. You aren’t “spending” them, they just reduce the price you must pay.
Thanks everyone for answering this.  It is clear that development cards can/ perhaps must be utilized first.  We're going to play this way from now on and.  Should be an interesting comparison.
Chip hoarding is part of the game in a way. It can force players to start buying cards when the chips they want are not available.

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!
I don’t think you’re reading “must” quite correctly there. Often when translating from German to English, there’s some confusion between “must” and “have to”. (“Musst nicht” in German is often mistranslated “must not” but actually means “do not have to”.)

In this case I think it means you need to spend only one green token. If a player wanted to spend more tokens in that case I don’t think the rules are intended to disallow it. But I can’t imagine why anyone would want to!

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!

Aye, good play is to choke the supply so your opponent is screwed. So taking the full discount and holding onto your chips is always the better option.

The Dized tutorial for Splendor is now a.body:
We've just released the tutorial for the game: it's available on our mobile app and directly online if you're using a computer. As all Dized tutorial, it's meant to be used during your first game, teaching all players at the same time as they're playing, while adapting to their choices. 🤓
And for your next games, you can always use the interactive rules (still in the app or online) to quickly find the answer to any question you might have about a specific rule. 🙂
Please tell us what you think and feel free to give any feedback you might have – unlike a printed rulebook, it's quite easy to update… 😉
